en.extract:
The purple-gaped honeyeater (Lichenostomus cratitius) is a species of bird in the family Meliphagidae. It is endemic to semi-arid southern Australia, where it inhabits mallee, tall heath and associated low eucalypt woodland.


Description
The purple-gaped honeyeater is a medium-sized (16-19 centimetres) honeyeater which is generally grey-olive above and buffish yellow below. They have a patterned head, with a black eyestripe against a grey background and purple gape above a yellow streak on the throat and pointed yellow ear coverts. Juveniles are similar, but with duller facial patterns, slightly browner plumage, and a yellow gape and gape line. The Kangaroo Island subspecies is considered to be larger and darker than the mainland subspecies.
Similar species include yellow-plumed honeyeater and singing honeyeater.
The bird is also named Wattle-Cheeked Honeyeater in Birds of Australia vol 11 by Gregory Mathews


Call
The song is a clear toweet-toweet-toweet, followed by a high-pitched yep-yep-yep, often performed from a tall branch, including before dawn.


Distribution
They occur in disjunct populations across southern Australia, east from southern Western Australia, with the eastern population largely occurring south of the Murray River, and NSW forming the extreme north-east of its range. Despite its naturally disjunct populations, the purple-gaped honeyeater has very low genetic diversity across its range.


Ecology and behaviour
The main habitat type for purple-gaped honeyeater is mallee woodland and shrubland. This includes bull mallee, with patches of green mallee or blue mallee. They also occur in yellow gum woodland with dense thickets of 'totem-poles' or violet honey-myrtle on low-lying flats and gullies in mallee areas. They are occasionally recorded in river red gums bordering waterways, and seasonally in red ironbark woodlands when they are in flower. Purple-gaped honeyeaters can also occasionally be found in gardens.
They are gregarious, usually seen in pairs, or groups of 3-8 birds. Generally considered quiet and unobtrusive, except in spring when they may call often. They are known to regularly drink and bathe, particularly in warmer weather. Purple-gaped honeyeaters associate with yellow-plumed honeyeater and tawny-crowned honeyeater, but can be aggressive towards other honeyeaters, and frequently chase other birds, particularly when feeding.


Diet
Purple-gaped honeyeaters feed mainly on nectar and insects, especially from flowering mallee eucalypts, and banksias. They also forage for insects under loose bark on trunks and branches of trees, or catch flying insects on the wing. Seeds, pollen and honeydew from scale insects  are less frequently consumed.


Reproduction
Purple-gaped honeyeaters build nests as a small cup of bark strips, grass and down, bound with spider web and egg sacs, slung in a horizontal fork or from slender branchlets within dense foliage (usually broombush or eucalypts), normally less than three metres above the ground.


Threats
The purple-gaped honeyeater is vulnerable to clearing of mallee, which destroys habitat by removing food plants and nesting sites.


Conservation actions


Conservation status
Internationally
* The species is listed under the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species as a species of 'Least concern'.
Australia
* In NSW, it is listed as Vulnerable under the Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016.
* In South Australia, the species is listed as Rare under the National Parks and Wildlife Act 1972.
* In Victoria, the species is listed as Vulnerable under the Flora and Fauna Guarantee Act 1988.


Protected areas
Purple-gaped honeyeaters occur in several protected areas, including:

New South Wales
* Mallee Cliffs National Park
South Australia
* Flinders Chase National Park
* Gawler Ranges National Park
* Billiatt Conservation Park
* Peebinga Conservation Park
* Gluepot Reserve
Victoria
* Greater Bendigo National Park
* Hattah-Kulkyne National Park
* Little Desert National Park
* Murray-Sunset National Park
* Wyperfeld National Park
* Big Desert Wilderness Park
* Lake Albacutya Park
* Inglewood Nature Conservation Reserve
* Wychitella Nature Conservation Reserve
Western Australia
* Cape Arid National Park
* Cape Le Grand National Park


References

The '''purple-gaped honeyeater''' ('''''Lichenostomus cratitius''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Meliphagidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to semi-arid southern [[Australia]], where it inhabits [[Mallee Woodlands and Shrublands|mallee]], tall heath and associated low eucalypt woodland.<ref name="ABG:RE">Menkhorst, P., Rogers, D., Clarke, R., Davies, J., Marsack, P., Franklin, K. (2019) ''The Australian Bird Guide: Revised Edition'', [[CSIRO Publishing]], Melbourne, Victoria, {{ISBN|9781486311934}}</ref>


==Description==
The purple-gaped honeyeater is a medium-sized (16-19 centimetres) honeyeater which is generally grey-olive above and buffish yellow below.<ref name="OEH"/> They have a patterned head, with a black eyestripe against a grey background and purple gape above a yellow streak on the throat and pointed yellow ear coverts.<ref name="OEH"/> Juveniles are similar, but with duller facial patterns, slightly browner plumage, and a yellow gape and gape line.<ref name="ABG:RE"/> The [[Kangaroo Island]] subspecies is considered to be larger and darker than the mainland subspecies.<ref name="ABG:RE"/>

Similar species include [[yellow-plumed honeyeater]] and [[singing honeyeater]].<ref name="WotBIC"/>
The bird is also named Wattle-Cheeked Honeyeater in Birds of Australia vol 11 by Gregory Mathews<ref>		https://anet.be/record/opacanet/c:lvd:6878811/N
</ref>

===Call===
The song is a clear ''toweet-toweet-toweet'', followed by a high-pitched ''yep-yep-yep'', often performed from a tall branch, including before dawn.<ref name="ABG:RE"/><ref name="WotBIC"/>

==Distribution==
They occur in disjunct populations across southern Australia, east from southern Western Australia, with the eastern population largely occurring south of the Murray River, and NSW forming the extreme north-east of its range.<ref name="OEH"/> Despite its naturally disjunct populations, the purple-gaped honeyeater has very low genetic diversity across its range.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Joseph, L.|author2=Campbell, C. D.|author3=Pedler, L. |author4=Drew, A. |name-list-style=amp|title=Genomic data show little geographical structure across the naturally fragmented range of the purple-gaped honeyeater|journal=Australian Journal of Zoology|date=2021|volume=67 |issue=4|pages=226–230|doi=10.1071/ZO20074|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Ecology and behaviour==
The main habitat type for purple-gaped honeyeater is mallee woodland and shrubland.<ref name="WotBIC">Tzaros, C. (2021) ''Wildlife of the Box-Ironbark Country.'' 2nd Edition, [[CSIRO Publishing]], Melbourne, Victoria, {{ISBN|9781486313150}}</ref> This includes [[Eucalyptus behriana|bull mallee]], with patches of [[Eucalyptus viridis|green mallee]] or [[Eucalyptus polybractea|blue mallee]].<ref name="WotBIC"/> They also occur in [[Eucalyptus leucoxylon|yellow gum]] woodland with dense thickets of [[Melaleuca decussata|'totem-poles']] or [[Melaleuca wilsonii|violet honey-myrtle]] on low-lying flats and gullies in mallee areas.<ref name="WotBIC"/> They are occasionally recorded in [[Eucalyptus camaldulensis|river red gums]] bordering waterways,<ref name="OEH"/> and seasonally in [[Eucalyptus tricarpa|red ironbark]] woodlands when they are in flower.<ref name="WotBIC"/> Purple-gaped honeyeaters can also occasionally be found in gardens.<ref name="ABG:RE"/>

They are gregarious, usually seen in pairs, or groups of 3-8 birds.<ref name="WotBIC"/> Generally considered quiet and unobtrusive, except in spring when they may call often.<ref name="WotBIC"/> They are known to regularly drink and bathe, particularly in warmer weather.<ref name="WotBIC"/> Purple-gaped honeyeaters associate with [[yellow-plumed honeyeater]] and [[tawny-crowned honeyeater]],<ref name="WotBIC"/> but can be aggressive towards other honeyeaters,<ref name="ABG:RE"/> and frequently chase other birds, particularly when feeding.<ref name="OEH"/>

===Diet===
Purple-gaped honeyeaters feed mainly on nectar and insects,<ref name="ABG:RE"/> especially from flowering mallee eucalypts,<ref name="WotBIC"/> and banksias.<ref name="OEH"/> They also forage for insects under loose bark on trunks and branches of trees, or catch flying insects on the wing.<ref name="WotBIC"/><ref name="OEH"/> Seeds, pollen and honeydew from [[Scale insect|scale insects]]  are less frequently consumed.<ref name="OEH"/>

===Reproduction===
Purple-gaped honeyeaters build nests as a small cup of bark strips, grass and down, bound with spider web and egg sacs, slung in a horizontal fork or from slender branchlets within dense foliage (usually [[Melaleuca uncinata|broombush]] or eucalypts), normally less than three metres above the ground.<ref name="OEH"/>

==Threats==
The purple-gaped honeyeater is vulnerable to clearing of mallee, which destroys habitat by removing food plants and nesting sites.<ref name="OEH"/>

==Conservation actions==
===Conservation status===
* '''Internationally'''
: * The species is listed under the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN Red List of Threatened Species]] as a species of 'Least concern'.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021"/> 

* '''Australia'''
: * In NSW, it is listed as ''Vulnerable'' under the [[Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016 (NSW)|Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016]].<ref name="OEH">{{cite web |url=https://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/threatenedspeciesapp/profile.aspx?id=10476 |title=Purple-gaped Honeyeater - profile |work=NSW Office of Environment & Heritage | access-date = 6 June 2022}}</ref> 
: * In South Australia, the species is listed as ''Rare'' under the [[Protected_areas_of_South_Australia#National_Parks_and_Wildlife_Act_1972|National Parks and Wildlife Act 1972]].<ref name="NPWA72">{{cite web|date=March 2021|title=National Parks and Wildlife Act 1972 |url=https://www.legislation.sa.gov.au/__legislation/lz/c/a/national%20parks%20and%20wildlife%20act%201972/current/1972.56.auth.pdf|type=PDF|publisher=[[Department for Environment and Water (South Australia)]]|access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>
: * In Victoria, the species is listed as ''Vulnerable'' under the [[Flora and Fauna Guarantee Act 1988]].<ref name="FFG1988">{{cite web|date=June 2021|title=Flora and Fauna Guarantee Act 1988 - Threatened List |url=https://www.environment.vic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0031/533893/20210714-FFG-Threatened-List.pdf|type=PDF|publisher=[[Department of Environment, Land, Water and Planning]]|access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>

===Protected areas===
Purple-gaped honeyeaters occur in several [[Protected area|protected areas]], including:
* '''New South Wales'''
: * [[Mallee Cliffs National Park]]<ref name="OEH"/>

* '''South Australia'''
: * [[Flinders Chase National Park]]<ref>{{cite journal|author=Paton, D. C.|name-list-style=amp|title=The importance of manna, honeydew and lerp in the diets of honeyeaters|journal=Emu|date=1980|volume=80 |pages=213–226|doi=10.1071/MU9800213 |doi-broken-date=22 September 2025 }}</ref> 
: * [[Gawler Ranges National Park]]<ref>{{cite web|title=Important Bird Areas factsheet: Gawler Ranges|url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/site/factsheet/gawler-ranges-iba-australia|publisher=BirdLife International|access-date=7 June 2022|date=2016}}</ref>
: * [[Billiatt Conservation Park]]<ref>{{cite web|title=Important Bird Areas factsheet: Billiatt|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/index.php/site/factsheet/billiatt-iba-australia|publisher=BirdLife International|access-date=7 June 2022|date=2009}}</ref>
: * [[Peebinga Conservation Park]]<ref>{{cite web|title=Important Bird Areas factsheet: Peebinga |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/index.php/site/factsheet/peebinga-iba-australia/text |publisher=BirdLife International |access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>
: * [[Gluepot Reserve]]<ref>{{cite web|work=Gluepot Reserve|date=April 2016|title=Birdlife Gluepot Reserve Bird List - April 2016 (Alphabetic Order) |url=https://www.gluepot.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Birdlist-April-2016-Alphabetic.pdf|type=PDF|publisher=[[Birdlife Australia]]|access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>

* '''Victoria'''
: * [[Greater Bendigo National Park]]<ref name="WotBIC"/>
: * [[Hattah-Kulkyne National Park]]<ref name="BotMP"/>
: * [[Little Desert National Park]]<ref name="BotMP"/>
: * [[Murray-Sunset National Park]]<ref name="BotMP">{{cite web|work=[[Parks Victoria]]|date=May 2019|title=Factsheet - Birds of the Mallee Parks |url=https://www.parks.vic.gov.au/-/media/project/pv/main/parks/documents/visitor-guides-and-publications/murray-sunset/factsheet-birds-of-the-mallee-parks.pdf?rev=51e70359ab6f4a68a421d4d17f2beea8|type=PDF|publisher=[[Victoria State Government]]|access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>
: * [[Wyperfeld National Park]]<ref name="BotMP"/>
: * [[Big Desert Wilderness Park]]<ref name="BotMP"/>
: * [[Lake Albacutya|Lake Albacutya Park]]<ref name="BotMP"/>
: * [[Inglewood Nature Conservation Reserve]]<ref name="WotBIC"/>
: * [[Wychitella Nature Conservation Reserve]]<ref name="WotBIC"/>

* '''Western Australia'''
: * [[Cape Arid National Park]]<ref>{{cite web|work=Parks and Wildlife Service|date=2017|title=Mt Ragged Campground |url=https://parks.dpaw.wa.gov.au/site/mt-ragged-campground|type=PDF|publisher=[[Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions (Western Australia)]]|access-date=7 June 2022}}</ref>
: * [[Cape Le Grand National Park]]<ref>{{cite web|title=Cape Le Grand National Park Bird Checklist |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/checklist.jsp?region=AUwa0012&list=howardmoore |publisher=[[Avibase]] |access-date=8 June 2022}}</ref>
